summary: Patrick McKenzie briefly acknowledges the growing attention his A/Bingo Rails split testing framework has received from prominent Ruby on Rails community sites. He reflects that naming the tool with a pun tied to his bingo card business may have been limiting, since the framework's capabilities extend well beyond that niche. He invites users to reach out with questions or feature requests.
key_points: A/Bingo gained visibility through coverage on the official Ruby on Rails blog and Rails Inside
McKenzie recognizes that a punny name tied to his niche product may undersell the tool's broader utility
Open invitation for community feedback signals his commitment to maintaining the open source project
money_quotes: Next time, incorporating a pun into the name is fine, but incorporating a pun more limiting than the actual feature set is perhaps not a good idea.
key_insight: When naming a product, a clever pun can backfire if it constrains people's perception of what the product actually does.
